content.problemStatement:
MoEngage has 333 decks. Each deck consists of NNN cards, numbered from 111 to NNN. He draws out 111 card from each deck randomly with each card having an equal probability of being drawn.

MoEngage drew cards numbered AAA and BBB from the decks 111 and 222 respectively. Now, he wonders what is the probability that he will end up with a funny hand after drawing the third card.

A funny hand is when 333 **consecutive** numbered cards are present in your hand.

Help MoEngage calculate the probability of ending up with a funny hand after drawing the third card.

If the final probability of ending up with a funny hand is PPP, you need to print the value P⋅NP\cdot NP⋅N. It can be shown that this value is an integer.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains a single line of input consisting of three space-separated integers N,A,N, A,N,A, and BBB denoting the size of each deck, the card drawn from the first deck, and the card drawn from the second deck respectively.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer, denoting P⋅NP\cdot NP⋅N on a separate line.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains three space‑separated integers N, A and B — the size of each deck and the numbers drawn from the first two decks respectively.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line: the value P·N, where P is the probability that after drawing the third card the three cards contain three consecutive numbers. It is guaranteed that this value is an integer.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: A=2, B=3 are consecutive. Possible third cards are 1 and 4, both inside [1,4]. So 2 favorable outcomes, P·N = 2.
Test case 2: A=2, B=1 are consecutive. Only card 3 makes a consecutive triple, so answer 1.
Test case 3: A=2, B=5 are three apart, no single card can complete a length‑3 consecutive segment, answer 0.

solution.approach:
Only the relative positions of A and B matter. Let a = min(A,B) and b = max(A,B). Let d = b - a.
- If d == 0 the two drawn cards are identical; with only one card left we can never obtain three distinct consecutive numbers → answer 0.
- If d == 1 the two cards are consecutive (a, a+1). To form a length‑3 consecutive segment we need either a‑1 or a+2. Each of them is valid only if it lies inside [1, N]. The number of valid choices is the answer.
- If d == 2 the two cards are the ends of a length‑3 segment (a, a+2). The missing middle card a+1 always lies in the range, so exactly one choice works → answer 1.
- If d > 2 the gap is too large; no single extra card can close the gap → answer 0.
Since the third card is drawn uniformly from N possibilities, the probability is (answer)/N and the required output P·N equals the answer itself. The algorithm is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.
